title: Unit 4 HCIIIA — All NGN Types
description: GI, Meningitis, Shingles, BPH, Peritonitis, Crohn's, UC — NGN format

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client with a bowel obstruction. Which nursing action is the highest priority?

options.A: Ambulate the client in the hallway twice per day
options.B: Insert and maintain a nasogastric tube for decompression
options.D: Encourage oral fluids to prevent dehydration

questions.rationale: Inserting an NG tube for decompression is the priority — it relieves pressure from the obstruction and prevents further complications including perforation. Oral fluids and ambulation are contraindicated with active obstruction.

questions.text: A client newly diagnosed with Crohn's disease asks the nurse what foods commonly trigger flares. Which foods should the nurse instruct the client to avoid? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: High-fiber, raw foods, carbonated beverages, spicy foods, and dairy commonly worsen Crohn's symptoms. Oatmeal (bland, soluble fiber) and lean poultry are generally well tolerated.

questions.case_study: A 72-year-old male with a history of BPH presents to the ED with inability to void for 14 hours, suprapubic pain rated 8/10, a palpable bladder to the umbilicus, and BP 158/94 mmHg. He reports his symptoms have been worsening over the past 3 days with increased straining and a weak urine stream.
questions.text: Complete the bowtie diagram. Select the most likely condition causing the client's presentation, the TWO priority nursing actions to take, and the TWO parameters to monitor after interventions are initiated.

condition_options: Acute urinary retention secondary to BPH
Renal calculi causing bilateral ureteral obstruction
Neurogenic bladder from new spinal cord injury
actions_to_take: Insert an indwelling urinary catheter (14–16 Fr) to drain the bladder
Administer IV furosemide to force diuresis
Notify the provider and prepare for catheterization
Obtain a urine culture and start empiric antibiotics
parameters_to_monitor: Urine output volume and color post-catheterization
Serum creatinine and BUN for renal function
Blood pressure trend after bladder decompression

correct_answers.condition: Acute urinary retention secondary to BPH
actions: Insert an indwelling urinary catheter (14–16 Fr) to drain the bladder
Notify the provider and prepare for catheterization
parameters: Urine output volume and color post-catheterization
Blood pressure trend after bladder decompression

questions.rationale: The classic presentation of acute urinary retention in BPH: 14-hour anuria, suprapubic pain, palpable bladder. Priority actions are notifying the provider and inserting a urethral catheter. Furosemide does not address mechanical obstruction. Monitor urine output and BP after decompression — post-obstructive diuresis and hypotension are known complications.

questions.case_study: A 34-year-old female is admitted with a 3-day history of fever (103.1°F), severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, and a petechial rash on her trunk and extremities. CSF analysis shows cloudy fluid, WBC 4,800/mm³ (neutrophil predominant), protein 180 mg/dL, glucose 28 mg/dL. Blood cultures are pending.
questions.text: For each assessment finding below, indicate whether the finding is consistent with Bacterial Meningitis, Viral Meningitis, or Both.
questions.rationale: Petechial rash is specific to meningococcal (bacterial) meningitis. Neutrophilic pleocytosis >1000 and low CSF glucose are hallmarks of bacterial infection. Headache, nuchal rigidity, and meningeal signs (Kernig's, Brudzinski's) occur in both types.

questions.case_study: A 58-year-old male post-laparoscopic appendectomy (Day 2) is being monitored by the nurse. The following data has been collected over the shift.
questions.text: Review the client's assessment data over time. For each parameter, indicate whether the trend is Improving, Worsening, or No change.
questions.rationale: This client is showing signs of developing sepsis — rising fever, tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria, and a markedly rising WBC all indicate worsening systemic infection. Only pain is improving. This constellation requires immediate provider notification and sepsis protocol activation.

questions.case_study: A client with a history of GERD has been prescribed omeprazole 20 mg PO daily. The nurse is completing discharge teaching.
questions.text: Complete the nurse's discharge teaching statement by selecting the best option for each blank.

sentence_parts: The nurse instructs the client to take omeprazole

sentence_parts.label: Timing
options: with food to reduce nausea
30 minutes before the first meal of the day
at bedtime to reduce overnight acid
with antacids to enhance absorption

sentence_parts: . The nurse explains that long-term use of this medication increases the risk of

sentence_parts.label: Risk
options: hyperkalemia and fluid retention
hypomagnesemia and vitamin B12 deficiency
hypercalcemia and kidney stones
iron toxicity and hemolysis

sentence_parts: . If the client develops severe diarrhea with cramping and mucus, the nurse should suspect

sentence_parts.label: Complication
options: GERD rebound hypersecretion
Clostridium difficile colitis
small bowel obstruction
acute pancreatitis

correct_answers.b1: 30 minutes before the first meal of the day

questions.rationale: PPIs must be taken 30 min before meals to inhibit active proton pumps. Long-term PPI use depletes magnesium and B12. C. difficile is a well-established complication of PPI use due to altered gastric pH allowing colonization.

questions.tier1_fact: Semi-Fowler's position is used in peritonitis to promote drainage and lung expansion
questions.text: The nurse is caring for a client with peritonitis. Which position is most appropriate?

options.A: Prone position to reduce abdominal pressure
options.C: Semi-Fowler's to promote drainage and lung expansion
options.D: Supine with legs elevated to prevent shock

questions.rationale: Semi-Fowler's position promotes downward drainage of peritoneal fluid to the pelvis and facilitates lung expansion. The prone and Trendelenburg positions are contraindicated in peritonitis.

questions.text: A client is admitted with shingles (herpes zoster). Which nursing interventions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options.A: Implement Airborne AND Contact precautions until lesions are fully crusted
options.B: Administer acyclovir as prescribed within 72 hours of rash onset
options.C: Educate the client that they can spread shingles to others
options.D: Apply cool compresses and calamine lotion for comfort
options.E: Place the client in a positive-pressure isolation room
options.F: Assess for involvement of the eye (herpes zoster ophthalmicus)

questions.rationale: Shingles requires Airborne + Contact precautions. Antivirals must be started within 72 hours. Comfort measures include cool compresses. Ophthalmic involvement is a serious complication requiring immediate ophthalmology consult. The client transmits chickenpox (not shingles) to susceptible contacts, and isolation rooms for shingles are negative pressure (not positive).

questions.case_study: A 28-year-old female with a 5-year history of Crohn's disease presents with a 3-week history of perianal pain, purulent drainage from a perianal opening, fever of 101.4°F, and elevated WBC of 17,200/mm³. She reports her Crohn's has been poorly controlled. CT scan confirms a perianal fistula with adjacent abscess.
questions.text: Complete the bowtie. Select the most likely condition, TWO priority nursing actions to take, and TWO parameters to monitor with proper management.

condition_options: Perianal fistula with abscess formation secondary to Crohn's disease
Clostridium difficile colitis with perirectal involvement
actions_to_take: Apply a heating pad to the perianal area for pain relief
Monitor and protect perianal skin from fistula drainage using barrier cream
Administer prescribed antibiotics (metronidazole/ciprofloxacin) and biologic agents
Encourage high-fiber diet to harden stools
Prepare client for surgical drainage/fistulotomy as ordered
parameters_to_monitor: Amount and character of fistula drainage
Skin integrity of the perianal area

correct_answers.condition: Perianal fistula with abscess formation secondary to Crohn's disease
actions: Monitor and protect perianal skin from fistula drainage using barrier cream
Administer prescribed antibiotics (metronidazole/ciprofloxacin) and biologic agents
parameters: Amount and character of fistula drainage

questions.rationale: Crohn's disease classically causes transmural inflammation leading to fistula and abscess formation. Priority nursing actions are skin protection (fistula drainage is enzymatically destructive) and administering antibiotics/biologics. Monitor drainage character and infection markers to evaluate treatment response.

questions.case_study: The nurse is reviewing medications for clients on the GI unit.
questions.text: For each medication listed, indicate the correct nursing priority action.

rows.label: Metronidazole — client reports tingling in hands/feet
Loperamide — client has fever 102°F and bloody diarrhea
Ciprofloxacin — client took calcium supplement 30 min ago
Omeprazole — client's magnesium is 1.6 mEq/L (low normal)

questions.rationale: Metronidazole peripheral neuropathy = hold. Loperamide with infectious diarrhea = absolute contraindication. Extended-release tablets cannot be crushed (releases entire dose at once). Ciprofloxacin binds calcium = absorption impaired, delay dose. PPI with low-normal magnesium = give but monitor trend.

questions.case_study: A 19-year-old male is admitted for bacterial meningitis. He is receiving ceftriaxone IV and dexamethasone. The nurse reviews data from the past 18 hours.
questions.text: For each parameter, select whether the trend indicates Improving, Worsening, or No change.
questions.rationale: Antibiotics and dexamethasone are working — fever, GCS, meningeal signs, and hemodynamics are all improving. The rash spread from admission to 6 hours but stabilized — representing 'no change' in the most recent interval, which is what trend questions assess.

questions.case_study: A post-operative client following a TURP (transurethral resection of the prostate) has a 3-way urinary catheter with continuous bladder irrigation (CBI) running at 200 mL/hr. At 0800, the nurse notes the drainage bag contains 1,400 mL of pink-tinged fluid. At 1200, total drainage is 2,600 mL. The irrigation infused during this period was 800 mL.
questions.text: Complete the nursing documentation by selecting the correct values for each blank.

sentence_parts: During the 4-hour period from 0800 to 1200, the total urine output (true urine, excluding irrigation) is

sentence_parts.label: True urine output
options: 400 mL
800 mL

sentence_parts: . The nurse assesses the drainage color as pink-tinged and determines this is

sentence_parts.label: Clinical significance
options: an emergency requiring immediate provider notification
expected in the first 24–48 hours post-TURP
a sign of bladder perforation
indicative of catheter displacement

sentence_parts: . Because the urine output is below 30 mL/hr, the nurse should first

sentence_parts.label: Priority action
options: increase the CBI rate and reassess in 1 hour
check for catheter kinks, clots, and patency before notifying the provider
clamp the catheter for 15 minutes to allow the bladder to fill
administer IV furosemide to force diuresis

correct_answers.b2: expected in the first 24–48 hours post-TURP
correct_answers.b3: check for catheter kinks, clots, and patency before notifying the provider

questions.rationale: True urine = Total drainage − Irrigation infused = (2600−1400) − 800 = 1200−800 = 400 mL over 4 hours (100 mL/hr — acceptable but on the low end). Pink-tinged urine is normal post-TURP. Low output requires checking catheter patency first before any pharmacologic intervention.

questions.tier1_fact: Abrupt TPN discontinuation causes rebound hypoglycemia — bridge with D10W at the same rate
questions.text: A client receiving TPN has the infusion accidentally discontinued. No new TPN bag is immediately available. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.A: Restart the TPN at a lower rate until the new bag arrives
options.B: Infuse D10W (10% dextrose) at the same rate as the TPN
options.C: Give the client orange juice orally to prevent hypoglycemia
options.D: Notify the provider and hold all IV fluids until TPN is available

questions.rationale: Abrupt TPN discontinuation causes rebound hypoglycemia because the pancreas continues secreting insulin for the high glucose load. D10W at the same rate maintains glucose delivery until TPN is restored. Oral intake is inappropriate for TPN patients.

questions.text: The nurse is preparing to discharge a client following an appendectomy for a ruptured appendix. Which instructions should the nurse include? Select all that apply.

options.A: Report fever above 101°F to the provider immediately
options.B: Resume normal diet and activity within 24 hours of discharge
options.C: Keep the incision site clean and dry; report redness, swelling, or drainage
options.D: Return to the ED for sudden worsening abdominal pain or abdominal rigidity
options.E: Complete the full course of prescribed antibiotics even if feeling better
options.F: Lift nothing heavier than 10 pounds until cleared by the provider

questions.rationale: All discharge instructions are correct except B — activity and diet are gradually resumed, not immediately. All other instructions address wound healing, infection signs, antibiotic adherence, and activity restrictions appropriately.

questions.interference_pair: Peptic Ulcer Disease vs Acute MI
questions.case_study: A 66-year-old male with a history of GERD and PUD presents to the ED with sudden onset severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, coffee-ground emesis, BP 88/54 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, Hgb 6.8 g/dL, BUN 42 mg/dL (elevated), and a positive NG lavage for bright red blood.
questions.text: Complete the bowtie diagram for this client's presentation.

condition_options: Acute MI with referred epigastric pain
Upper GI bleed secondary to perforated peptic ulcer
actions_to_take: Establish two large-bore IV accesses and initiate aggressive fluid resuscitation
Insert NG tube and perform gastric lavage as ordered
Place client on NPO status and prepare for emergent upper endoscopy
Administer oral antacids to neutralize gastric acid
Transfuse packed red blood cells as ordered and monitor hemodynamic response
parameters_to_monitor: Blood pressure and heart rate response to fluid resuscitation
Gastric lavage output color and volume

correct_answers.condition: Upper GI bleed secondary to perforated peptic ulcer
actions: Establish two large-bore IV accesses and initiate aggressive fluid resuscitation
Transfuse packed red blood cells as ordered and monitor hemodynamic response
parameters: Blood pressure and heart rate response to fluid resuscitation

questions.rationale: Bright red blood in NG lavage, coffee-ground emesis, hemodynamic instability, and low Hgb confirm a massive upper GI bleed. Priority = hemodynamic stabilization with IV access + fluids + PRBCs. Monitor BP/HR and Hgb/Hct to assess resuscitation effectiveness. Oral antacids are completely inappropriate in hemorrhagic shock.

questions.case_study: The nurse is reviewing the chart of four clients on the medical-surgical unit.
questions.text: For each client finding, select whether it requires Immediate Action, Monitoring Only, or Expected/Normal Finding.

rows.label: Post-TURP client: urine drainage is bright red with clots
Post-laparoscopic cholecystectomy: right shoulder pain Day 1
Client on mesalamine: WBC 2,800/mm³ with bruising and sore throat
Client with meningitis: GCS drops from 14 to 9 over 2 hours
Client with Crohn's post-ileostomy: green liquid output Day 2
Client on ciprofloxacin: reports new bilateral heel pain

questions.rationale: Bright red clots post-TURP = possible arterial bleed → increase CBI and notify. Shoulder pain post-lap chole = expected CO₂ referred pain. Mesalamine + bone marrow suppression signs = hold and notify. Declining GCS in meningitis = imminent herniation. Green ileostomy output = normal early post-op. Bilateral tendon pain on fluoroquinolones = tendon rupture warning → hold immediately.

questions.case_study: A 52-year-old female with ulcerative colitis (UC) is admitted during a severe flare. She is receiving IV methylprednisolone and bowel rest. The nurse monitors the following data.
questions.text: For each parameter, identify whether the trend is Improving, Worsening, or No change.
questions.rationale: While stool frequency is decreasing (improving response to steroids), the development of worsening abdominal distension, absent bowel sounds, rising fever, and declining albumin are signs of toxic megacolon — a life-threatening emergency. This mixed picture is the classic NGN clinical judgment challenge: not all trends point the same direction.

questions.case_study: A 45-year-old male with a new diagnosis of bacterial meningitis secondary to Neisseria meningitidis is admitted. Blood cultures were drawn. He has a penicillin allergy documented as 'anaphylaxis.'
questions.text: Complete the nursing actions by selecting the best option for each blank.

sentence_parts: The nurse knows that bacterial meningitis caused by N. meningitidis requires

sentence_parts.label: Isolation type
options: Airborne precautions in a negative pressure room
Droplet precautions — surgical mask within 3 feet
Contact precautions with gown and gloves only
Standard precautions only

sentence_parts: for the first 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy. Because the client has a documented anaphylaxis to penicillin, the nurse anticipates the provider will order

sentence_parts.label: Antibiotic choice
options: amoxicillin-clavulanate as the first-line agent
chloramphenicol or meropenem as a penicillin-safe alternative
ceftriaxone, which is safe in penicillin allergy
vancomycin, which is first-line for all meningitis

sentence_parts: . Prior to the first antibiotic dose, the nurse must ensure

sentence_parts.label: Pre-antibiotic action
options: the client has eaten a full meal to protect the GI tract
blood cultures have already been collected
a lumbar puncture has been completed and results reviewed
the client has signed a consent form for antibiotic therapy

correct_answers.b1: Droplet precautions — surgical mask within 3 feet
correct_answers.b2: chloramphenicol or meropenem as a penicillin-safe alternative
correct_answers.b3: blood cultures have already been collected

questions.rationale: N. meningitidis spreads via respiratory droplets, not airborne route. With penicillin anaphylaxis, cephalosporins carry a cross-reactivity risk — meropenem or chloramphenicol are safe alternatives. Blood cultures MUST be drawn before antibiotics to identify the organism, but antibiotics should not be delayed for LP results.
